text.title: Remote and Async Ceremony Patterns
text.summary: Timezone-aware ceremony design: async standup constraints, distributed team rules, ceremony health measurement.

rule.statement: Async standup prompts must focus on goal-progress and blockers, not "what did you do yesterday". Reject prompts that produce individual status reports.
rule.rationale: Status questions produce status answers; goal-progress questions keep the team aligned on the sprint goal rather than individual task lists.

rule.statement: Use tz-aware scheduling for async standups; recalculate after daylight-saving transitions. Naive UTC offsets break twice a year.
rule.rationale: A standup that works in March may exclude APAC or Eastern Europe after the DST shift. Encode acceptable hours per participant in the bot config.

rule.statement: Maximum one sync ceremony per day for distributed teams; Sprint Planning and Retrospective may share a day, but no more than two sync events back-to-back.
rule.rationale: Zoom fatigue compounds across sessions; more than one long sync per day collapses participation quality by the second event.

rule.statement: Any global recurring meeting must either rotate its time across quarters or run two sessions. Document the rotation schedule in the team calendar.
rule.rationale: A fixed "Tue 10:00 UTC" permanently excludes APAC time zones from meaningful participation.

rule.statement: Run a ceremony health survey (1-5 scale) every quarter. Track: time-box compliance, sprint-goal-met rate, retro action closure rate, velocity stability. Trigger a retro on ceremonies if any metric scores below 3.
rule.rationale: Without measurement, ceremony decay is invisible until the team openly refuses to attend. Quantified health scores make the problem surfaceable before that point.

antipattern.description: Ceremony rituals re-imported when a team has moved to flow-based delivery.
antipattern.reason: Sprint cadence imposed on a Kanban team adds overhead without benefit; the team "does Scrum ceremonies for Kanban" and cargo-cults both methods badly.
